pages.title: Visual Overview
pages.contentHtml:
Visual Overview

Objective: To introduce the role and operating environment of the senior financial executive.

pages.title: 1.1.1 To Achieve Organisational Goals
pages.contentHtml:
1.1 To Achieve Organisational Goals

A company will usually have a vision, whether articulated or not, that is the foundation of the company and should set out its direction and values.

The corporate mission should set out what the organisation exists to do and establish clear company aims and goals.

Definition
Strategy – a plan of action designed to achieve a long-term organisational goal or aim.

A corporate strategy might ask:

  • What business should we be in?
    • What are our special organisational abilities through which we can create shareholder value?
    • What business sector are these abilities best applied to?
  • How should we allocate our various capitals to our business(es)?
  • How can we best design our organisation? Should we be centralised or decentralised?
  • How should we manage risk and return and create incentives?

A business strategy might ask:

  • What corporate culture will best assist in achieving our aims?
  • How should we compete?
  • How should we organise our strategic business units?

An operational strategy might ask:

  • How should our strategic business units operate?
  • What functions should be outsourced?
  • What incentive schemes should be offered to staff?

pages.title: 1.1.2 Financial Management
pages.contentHtml:
1.2 Financial Management

The objective of a financial strategy is to achieve a financial goal or aim and usually includes the utilisation and management of financial resources through the process of financial management. Financial strategies include:

  • The investment decision;
  • The financing decision;
  • The dividend decision;
  • Risk management.

Strategies can be long or short term. For example, liquidity management is a short-term financial strategy, whereas gearing and dividend decisions are long term.

A financial resource is money available to a business (e.g. cash, borrowing capacity, investments or credit lines). Financial resources are used with the other capitals (human, intellectual, etc) to achieve strategic objectives. Managing "financial resources" means managing the company's assets and liabilities, whether monetary or nonmonetary.

Strategies may be expressed as plans, statements, budgets and forecasts (including cash flow forecasts). The achievement of strategies requires internal controls. Collectively, such strategies and controls amount to the management of financial resources.

Non-financial strategies include human resources (HR), marketing, product development, etc.

pages.title: 1.2.1 Corporate Objectives in Practice
pages.contentHtml:
2.1 Corporate Objectives in Practice

In practice, companies are likely to have a variety of different objectives, which may include a number of the following:

  • profit targets;
  • market share targets;
  • share price growth;
  • local and environmental concerns;
  • contented workforce;
  • meeting short-term targets; and
  • long-term plans.

These objectives can be classified as follows:

  • Profit goals – objectives which lead directly to increased profits (e.g. cost reduction measures);
  • Surrogate profit goals – objectives which lead indirectly to increased profits (e.g. maintaining a contented workforce);
  • Constraints on profit – objectives which restrict profit (e.g. ensuring that the company's operations do not harm the environment);
  • Dysfunctional goals – objectives which do not provide a benefit even in the long run (e.g. the pursuit of market leadership at all costs).

A company may aim at either maximising or satisficing these objectives:

  • Maximising involves seeking the best possible outcome;
  • Satisficing involves finding an adequate outcome.

Criticisms of profit maximisation include the following:

  • Maximising shareholder wealth ignores the interests of other stakeholders such as employees, customers and arguably, society.
  • In the case of unlisted companies, even the shareholders may not require maximised returns. For example, some closely held companies are run as "lifestyle firms" whose main objective is to provide their owners with the opportunity to earn a living while pursuing a particular lifestyle.

pages.title: 1.2.2 Maximisation of Shareholder Wealth
pages.contentHtml:
2.2 Maximisation of Shareholder Wealth

Maximising shareholder wealth is a single corporate objective that can help managers in decision-making.

Shareholder wealth is the combination of dividend and share price growth, referred to as Total Shareholder Returns (TSR).

The objective of maximising shareholder wealth can be justified in the following ways:

  • The company that provides the highest returns for its investors will find it easiest to raise new finance and grow. If a company does not provide competitive returns, it will inevitably decline.
  • The directors of a company have a legal duty to run the company on behalf of the shareholders. It is generally considered a reasonable assumption that the shareholders of listed companies (mainly institutional investors) seek to maximise wealth.
Activity 1 NPV, Payback and Time

Required:

Discuss whether the maximisation of net present value (NPV) will always maximise shareholder's wealth, including reference to payback and the issue of long- versus short-term returns.

pages.title: 1.2.3 Communication to Stakeholders
pages.contentHtml:
2.3 Communication to Stakeholders

Effective communication and communication strategy are essential to implementing financial policies and realising corporate goals. Communications to stakeholders must take into account:

  • Whether they are internal or external stakeholders;
  • How they rank in terms of power over, and level of interest in, the entity's activities;
  • The purpose of the communication;
  • The most appropriate medium.
Exam advice

Question 1 in the exam typically requires a report to a board of directors. Marks for the professional skill of communication will be available for:

  • General report format and structure;
  • Style, language and clarity;
  • Effectiveness of communication.

pages.title: 1.3.1 Agency Theory
pages.contentHtml:
3.1 Agency Theory

Agency theory examines the duties and conflicts that occur between the parties in a company that has an agency relationship, for example:

A company can be viewed as a set of contracts between these various interest groups. The company will only succeed if the interests of each group are met to at least an adequate level.

pages.title: 1.3.2 Stakeholders
pages.contentHtml:
3.2 Stakeholders

Companies are made up of a variety of different interest groups or stakeholders, all of whom are likely to have varying interests in and objectives for the company.

While shareholders are the primary stakeholder, modern corporate governance suggests that directors should bear in mind the objectives of a broader range of interested parties. Directors are therefore expected to show responsibility:

  • to creditors (e.g. reasonable payment terms);
  • to employees (e.g. health and safety); and
  • to customers (e.g. reliable products/services at a fair price);
  • to society (e.g. minimising pollution, investing in social projects).

Therefore, the corporate objective may become satisficing (i.e. producing satisfactory rather than maximum returns for stakeholders). With the rise of the "ethical investor" on world stock markets, many shareholders are willing to accept slightly lower returns in exchange for the companies they invest in following a wide range of both financial and non-financial objectives.

pages.title: 1.3.3 Directors and Shareholders
pages.contentHtml:
3.3 Directors and Shareholders

3.3.1 The Agency Issue

In larger companies, the shareholders entrust the management of the company to the directors – referred to as the separation of ownership and control. The directors are employed to manage the company on behalf of the shareholders and are duty-bound to promote the company's success.

Directors will have personal objectives, such as:

  • increasing personal remuneration levels;
  • maximising bonus payments;
  • empire building;
  • job security.

A small number of directors have been guilty of not fulfilling their duties by:

  • Creative accounting;
  • Hiding corporate activity (e.g. using special purpose vehicles);
  • Takeovers – protecting jobs rather than shareholder interests;
  • Disregard for environmental issues.

3.3.2 Agency Costs

If directors follow personal objectives which conflict with those of their shareholders, this leads to agency costs (i.e. lost potential returns for shareholders).

Definition
Corporate governance – the system by which companies are directed and controlled.

Good corporate governance procedures should be implemented to minimise the effect of agency costs.

pages.title: 1.3.4 Goal Congruence
pages.contentHtml:
3.4 Goal Congruence

Goal congruence is when the interests of stakeholders align with the best interests of the company as a whole.

Methods of encouraging goal congruence between directors and shareholders:

  • Executive Share Option Plans (ESOPs) – directors and shareholders will benefit from a rising share price.
  • Long-term incentive plans – paying directors a bonus if the company's performance is good over several years.
  • Transparency in corporate reporting – ensures accurate and comprehensive information.
  • Improved corporate governance (e.g. appointing truly independent non-executive directors).
  • Increased shareholder activism (e.g. using voting rights).

pages.title: 1.4.1 Ethical Contexts for the Financial Executive or Adviser
pages.contentHtml:
4.1 Ethical Contexts for the Financial Executive or Adviser

4.1.1 Main Principles

The main principles of ethical conduct are:

  • Act with integrity.
  • Be a credit to the profession.
  • Use independent professional judgement.
  • Be competent.

4.1.2 Fundamental Responsibilities

A senior financial executive/adviser should:

  • Know and comply with laws, regulations, ethical codes and professional standards.
  • Not knowingly participate or assist others in violating applicable regulations or ethical codes.

4.1.3 Relationship with the Profession

A senior financial executive/adviser should:

  • Use ACCA designation with dignity.
  • Not engage in any act that adversely reflects on one's honesty, trustworthiness or professional competence.

4.1.6 Compliance with Anti–Money-Laundering (AML) Legislation

Definition

Money laundering – the process by which the proceeds of crime are converted into assets which appear to have a legitimate origin.

pages.title: 1.4.2 Ethical Decision-Making
pages.contentHtml:
4.2 Ethical Decision-making

4.2.1 Rest's Model

This four-component model is as follows:

Step 1It must be recognised that there is a moral issue involved.
Step 2The decision-maker must be able to select an appropriate action.
Step 3The decision-maker must attach priority to moral values rather than self-interest.
Step 4The decision-maker must have sufficient moral strength to implement the resolution.

4.2.2 Albrecht's Ethical Development Model (EDM)

1. Personal ethical understanding

A foundation level. Appreciates the difference between right and wrong.

2. Application of ethics to business situations

Translating and applying ethics to the business context.

3. Ethical courage

Requires individuals to develop ethical strength and moral conviction.

4. Ethical leadership

The ethical leader can inspire others to develop their ethical awareness.

pages.title: 1.4.3 Ethical Decision-Making Models (EDMM)
pages.contentHtml:
4.3 Ethical Decision-making Models (EDMM)

4.3.2 Tucker's 5-Question Model

Tucker's model asks five questions about a business decision which must be answered in the affirmative to confirm that the decision is ethical. Is the decision:

  1. Profitable (But compared to what?)
  2. Legal (What framework was used?)
  3. Fair (From whose perspective? Consider stakeholders.)
  4. Right (Based on what ethical position?)
  5. Sustainable (Or environmentally sound?)

4.3.3 ACCA Ethical Conflict Resolution

Applying the ACCA's Code of Ethics and Conduct, professional accountants should consider:

  • the relevant facts;
  • the ethical issues involved;
  • related fundamental principles;
  • the organisation's established procedures;
  • the action that can be followed and the probable outcome;
  • alternative courses of action and their consequences; and
  • internal and external sources of consultation available.

pages.title: 1.4.4 Ethics: Agency Issues and Stakeholder Conflicts
pages.contentHtml:
4.4 Ethics: Agency Issues and Stakeholder Conflicts

4.4.1 Agency Issues and Ethics

Agency issues are particularly likely to affect a quoted company's ethical framework where there is significant divorce of ownership and control.

If the directors come under pressure from shareholders to relax the company's ethical guidelines, appropriate responses could include:

  • quoting relevant legislation which imposes penalties for contravention;
  • encouraging ethical investment funds to take shareholdings in the company.

4.4.2 Stakeholder Conflicts

Although shareholders are a company's primary stakeholder, there are also potential conflicts with other groups:

  • Consumers may boycott the company's products if made in exploitative conditions;
  • Governments may take action against aggressive tax avoidance schemes.

pages.title: 1.5.1 Sustainability
pages.contentHtml:
5.1 Sustainability

Definitions

Sustainability – a process of change in which the exploitation of resources, the direction of investments, the orientation of technological development and institutional change are made consistent with future as well as present needs.

Corporate sustainability – a business approach that creates long-term shareholder value by embracing opportunities and managing risks from economic, environmental and social developments.

Sustainable development – development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

Sustainable finance – the process of taking ESG considerations into account when making investment decisions.

5.1.2 ESG Investing

ESG investing prioritises ESG factors and performance alongside the profits of an organisation. It considers the material risks and opportunities businesses face related to these factors.

pages.title: 1.5.2 Triple Bottom-Line Reporting (TBL or 3BL)
pages.contentHtml:
5.2 Triple Bottom-Line Reporting (TBL or 3BL)

Triple bottom-line accounting attempts to measure and report corporate performance against economic, social and environmental benchmarks.

AdvantagesDisadvantages
  • Makes transparent decisions that consider effects on environment, people and financial capital.
  • More informed decision making.
  • Improved relationships with key stakeholders.
  • Specific commercial advantages.
  • Enhancement of reputation and brand.
  • Few standards for measuring these effects.
  • Significant variability in disclosure.
  • Increase in annual reporting costs.
  • Potential exposure to risk and liability.
  • Potential bias in voluntary presentation.

pages.title: 1.5.3 Modern Approaches to Reporting ESG Issues
pages.contentHtml:
5.3 Modern Approaches to Reporting ESG Issues

  • The Integrated Reporting Framework aims to provide a holistic view by showing the impact on six capitals.
  • The Task Force on Climate Related Disclosures (TCFD) issued climate disclosure recommendations.
  • The GRI issues voluntary global standards for sustainability transparency.
  • The International Sustainability Standards Board was set up by the IFRS foundation in November 2021. IFRS-S1 deals with general sustainability disclosures, while IFRS-S2 deals with climate related disclosures.
  • Governments may develop their own sustainability standards (e.g. the EU has mandatory standards for larger listed companies from 2024).

pages.title: 1.6.1 Need for Risk Management
pages.contentHtml:
6.1 Need for Risk Management

Definition
Risk – the variability (volatility) of returns, both positive and negative, that can be quantified through statistical measures.

6.1.1 Theory

Risk management is about decisions made to change the volatility of returns. Risk should be managed if it increases the value of a company.

6.1.2 In Practice

Implementing a default policy of hedging all risks is unlikely to be effective because:

  • Hedging may give short-run but not long-run protection.
  • Hedging incurs costs.
  • The shareholders may have already hedged many risks through portfolio diversification.

Why do many organisations engage in risk management?

  • Privately held companies – shareholders may have significant wealth in one company.
  • Management bonuses are exposed to total risk.
  • Progressive corporate tax systems – smoothing profits reduces tax burden.
  • Reduction in cost of capital.
  • Financial distress costs.

pages.title: 1.6.2 Conflicts Between Equity and Debt Investors
pages.contentHtml:
6.2 Conflicts Between Equity and Debt Investors

Equity investors absorb the company's level of business risk. Their downside risk is restricted by limited liability.

Debt investors receive fixed contractual payments. Their primary risk is default risk/credit risk.

The different positions can lead to differing attitudes towards risk-taking:

  • Shareholders may develop an "appetite" for risk.
    • If high-risk strategies succeed, equity rises in value.
    • If the gamble fails, shareholders can walk away under limited liability.
  • Debt investors would be highly risk averse as any fall in asset value would reduce recoverability of their debt.

pages.title: 1.6.3 Types of Risk and Risk Mitigation
pages.contentHtml:
6.3 Types of Risk and Risk Mitigation

6.3.1 Business and Financial Risk

Definition
Business risk – the risk associated with volatility of operating cash flows.

Business risk is driven by sales volatility and the level of operational gearing.

Definition
Financial risk – the risk associated with how a company finances its operations.

Financial risk concerns the volatility of returns to equity due to the priority of fixed interest payments on debt.

Definition
Credit (default) risk – the potential that a borrower will fail to meet its obligations.
Definition
Reputation risk – the risk of failure to meet stakeholder expectations.

6.3.2 Operational Risk

Definition
Operational risk – the risk of loss resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and systems or from external events.

pages.title: 1.6.4 Risk Mitigation, Hedging and Diversification
pages.contentHtml:
6.4 Risk Mitigation, Hedging and Diversification

  • Risk mitigation is any action or strategy to reduce the impact or likelihood of risks or threats.
  • Risk hedging is a financial strategy to reduce potential losses associated with adverse price movements.
  • Risk diversification refers to diversification either domestically into other industries or other countries.

Corporate diversification may be justified when:

  • significant synergy may be obtained through buying competitors, suppliers or distributors;
  • diversified groups have lower financial distress risk, may achieve an enhanced credit rating and may pay less tax.

pages.title: 1.6.5 Developing a Framework for Risk Management
pages.contentHtml:
6.5 Developing a Framework for Risk Management

6.5.1 Elements

A risk management process should incorporate:

pages.title: Syllabus Coverage
pages.contentHtml:
Syllabus Coverage

This chapter covers the following Learning Outcomes.

A. Role of the Senior Financial Adviser in the Multinational Organisation

  1. The role and responsibility of senior financial executive/adviser
  1. Develop strategies for the achievement of the company's goals.
  2. Recommend strategies for the management of financial resources.
  3. Advise the board in setting financial goals and policy development.
  1. Financial strategy formulation
  1. Explain the theoretical and practical rationale for risk management.
  2. Assess the organisation's exposure to business and financial risk.
  3. Develop a framework for risk management.
  1. Corporate ESG and ethical issues

pages.title: Summary and Quiz
pages.contentHtml:
Summary and Quiz

  • A strategy is a plan of action designed to achieve a long-term organisational goal or aim.
  • The objective of a financial strategy is to achieve a financial goal or aim.
  • A company may aim to maximise or satisfice corporate objectives.
  • Most traditional finance theory assumes a company's objective is to maximise shareholder wealth.
  • Directors should also consider other stakeholders and pursue non-financial objectives.
  • In practice, managers may have personal objectives that conflict with their responsibilities as agents, creating agency costs.
  • Well-designed remuneration systems and good corporate governance should reduce agency costs.
  • Corporate sustainability creates long-term shareholder value by embracing opportunities and managing risks.
  • Business risk is the risk associated with volatility of operating cash flows.
  • Financial risk is the risk associated with the company's capital structure.
Chapter 1 Quiz
